ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general scallop food product entry; no specific dried/dehydrated scallop entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated dried scallop code; A00LC (scallops) used as the closest defensible anchor with processing qualifier noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried scallop as a food substance; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried scallop used as a flavouring ingredient is approximately 5–10 g (roughly 1–2 small dried pieces), contributing intense umami flavour to soups, congee, and braised dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Scallop — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried scallop (cr_id F03040031). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Scallops, raw, SR Legacy NDB 15152) adjusted for dehydration concentration factor as a secondary approved source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate — negligible carbohydrate seafood protein; University of Sydney GI Database consulted, no direct entry for dried scallop
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for dried scallop. Dried scallop contains 7.6g carbohydrate per 100g, primarily as glycogen, but is consumed in very small flavouring quantities of 5–10g per serving, delivering approximately 0.4–0.8g carbohydrate per serving. High-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood products of this type are consistently assigned GI values in the very low range (approximately 5) in peer-reviewed literature, as the carbohydrate matrix is dominated by protein and the glycaemic response is negligible. GI is set to 5 (low category) as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 8g serving = (5 × 0.6) / 100 = 0.03, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 2500 mg per 100g
hypertension.guidance: Dried scallop must be restricted to very small flavouring quantities (5–10g per dish) in hypertensive patients, as even a 10g serving delivers 250mg sodium — nearly half the recommended daily limit for hypertension management; soaking in water prior to use and discarding the soaking liquid can reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (7.6g/100g) with negligible glycaemic impact at typical serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: At a typical 5–10g flavouring serving, dried scallop contributes less than 0.8g carbohydrate and has negligible glycaemic impact; however, its extreme sodium content (2500mg/100g) warrants strict quantity control, as high sodium intake is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and insulin resistance in type 2 diabetic patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (2500mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (150mg/100g) with low saturated fat (0.2g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The extreme sodium content of dried scallop poses a significant cardiovascular risk and its use should be limited to 5–10g as a flavouring agent; the low saturated fat and presence of marine-derived omega-3 precursors are partially favourable, but the sodium burden outweighs these benefits when consumed in larger quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium content worsening inflammatory tissue oedema; no confirmed omega-3 data to support anti-inflammatory classification
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried scallop should be used only in very small quantities (5–10g) as a flavouring in RA patients, as its sodium content of 2500mg/100g can significantly exacerbate joint swelling and fluid retention; the absence of confirmed omega-3 data means it cannot be classified as an anti-inflammatory food source.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried scallop (干贝, gan bei) is a traditional East Asian preserved seafood ingredient produced by boiling fresh scallops and then sun-drying or mechanically dehydrating them until they reach a firm, dense, golden-brown state. The dehydration process concentrates all nutrients — particularly protein (65.7g/100g) and sodium (2500mg/100g) — to very high levels. Dried scallop is prized in Chinese, Cantonese, and broader East Asian cuisines for its intensely savoury, sweet umami flavour, which is released slowly during cooking. It is commonly used as a flavouring agent in congee (rice porridge), braised dishes, soups such as Buddha Jumps Over the Wall (佛跳墙), and festive preparations. A typical culinary use involves soaking 5–10g of dried scallop in warm water for 30–60 minutes to rehydrate it before adding it to dishes; the soaking liquid is often retained and added to the dish for additional flavour. Nutritionally, dried scallop is an exceptionally high-protein, low-fat food, but its sodium content is clinically extreme at 2500mg/100g, making it unsuitable as a primary protein source for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is best treated as a condiment or flavouring ingredient rather than a main protein component. Cholesterol is moderate at 150mg/100g. Carbohydrate content is low at 7.6g/100g, primarily as glycogen, with negligible glycaemic impact at typical serving sizes. Dried scallop is a shellfish (mollusc) allergen and is not suitable for individuals with shellfish allergies.
common_user_queries: How do you use dried scallop in cooking?
Is dried scallop high in sodium?
Is dried scallop good for hypertension?
How much protein is in dried scallop?
Is dried scallop a shellfish allergen?

ingredient in turnip cake (lo bak go)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040031), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy dried scallop data as a secondary approved source. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a dehydrated shellfish product.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dried scallop not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; USDA FoodData Central does not have a sufficiently specific dried scallop entry with full fatty acid profile as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dried scallop not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item and no sufficiently specific approved secondary source was available to supply defensible values. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — marine shellfish products do not contain industrially produced trans fats). sugar_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — HPB source reports no sugar; scallop glycogen is not classified as free sugar). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — shellfish contain no dietary fibre). Glycemic index is set to 5 (low) as an analogous estimate: at a typical 8g serving, dried scallop delivers approximately 0.6g carbohydrate, making glycaemic load effectively 0.0; no direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for dried scallop; GI of 5 is a conservative analogous estimate consistent with high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood products in peer-reviewed literature. Saturated fat of 0.2g/100g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy (Scallops, raw, USDA NDB 15152) adjusted for dehydration concentration factor; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct dried scallop fatty acid analysis when available. The rheumatoid_arthritis health context from the input record has been preserved and refined in additional_conditions.
